steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank fills two slots: a noun describing the weavers who developed the style, and a time expression meaning ‘160 years in the past.’ Standard English requires (1) a plural, non-possessive noun for the weavers and (2) a plain plural years with no apostrophe, since the number is the object of ago, not a possessor.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "artist's over 160 year's": both words carry apostrophes. Artist’s is singular possessive (wrong number and wrong case) and year’s turns the time unit into a possessor, which is ungrammatical with ago. ✗
  • "artists over 160 years'": artists is correctly plural, but years’ is plural possessive. A time unit with ago is never possessive. ✗
  • "artists' over 160 years": years is correct here, but artists’ is plural possessive — nothing is possessed by the artists in this phrase. ✗
  • "artists over 160 years": artists is plural non-possessive and years is plain plural. Both forms are correct. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
